adjustments.recommendation: No dose adjustment required. Full dose: 1000mg BID acceptable.
Use with caution; limit dose to 1000mg/day; reassess regularly. Not recommended to initiate new treatment.
CONTRAINDICATED. Discontinue metformin. Risk of metformin-associated lactic acidosis (MALA).
No dose adjustment required.
Reduce total daily dose. Max 700mg TID (2100mg/day). Use lowest effective dose.
Max dose 300mg once daily. Current regimen of 300mg TID (900mg/day) significantly exceeds this threshold.
Max dose 150mg once daily. Hemodialysis patients: supplemental dose after each session.
Standard dosing. Monitor creatinine and potassium.
Start at 2.5-5mg/day. Titrate based on response. Monitor potassium, creatinine carefully.
Use with extreme caution; risk of acute kidney injury and severe hyperkalemia. Consider alternative.
Standard dosing. Higher doses may be needed as CKD progresses due to reduced tubular secretion.
May need significantly higher doses (2-4x) to achieve diuresis due to competition from uremic toxins for renal tubular secretion. Monitor electrolytes closely.
No formal dose adjustment based on eGFR, but CKD increases sensitivity to warfarin. Monitor INR more frequently (at least monthly). Risk of major bleeding is significantly increased in CKD Stage 4-5.
Standard dosing.
Reduce dose by 50%; use every 48 hours.
Reduce dose by 75%; use every 96 hours or consider alternative.
Standard dosing up to 300mg/day.
Reduce dose to 200mg/day.
Reduce dose to 100mg/day. Use lowest effective dose.
Use with caution; monitor potassium closely. Avoid with K+ > 5.0 mEq/L.
Avoid use. High risk of dangerous hyperkalemia in CKD Stage 4-5.
Use lower end of dosing range; monitor serum levels. Target 0.5-0.9 ng/mL in heart failure.
Significantly reduce dose (often 50%); extend dosing interval. Monitor serum levels closely. High risk of toxicity.
Standard dosing.
Reduce dose by 50% or extend interval to every 18-24 hours.
Metoprolol is hepatically metabolized; no renal dose adjustment required. However, hemodynamic monitoring recommended in severe CKD.
No renal dose adjustment required. Amlodipine is primarily hepatically metabolized.
Standard doses (81mg). Use with caution; increased GI and renal risks.
Avoid if possible; risk of sodium/water retention and further reduction in GFR.
Use with caution. Monitor serum potassium regularly, especially with concurrent ACE inhibitors or potassium-sparing diuretics.
Use with extreme caution or avoid. High risk of life-threatening hyperkalemia in CKD Stage 4-5, especially with concurrent ACE inhibitors (e.g., lisinopril). Monitor K+ at least weekly.
No renal dose adjustment required. Omeprazole is hepatically metabolized. However, long-term use in CKD may increase risk of interstitial nephritis.
Standard dosing. Monitor for myopathy; risk increases with CYP3A4 inhibitors.
Start at lowest dose (5-10mg). Risk of rhabdomyolysis significantly increased in severe CKD, especially with concurrent CYP3A4 inhibitors. Max 20mg/day.
Standard dosing.
Reduce dose by 50%. Fluconazole is primarily renally excreted (>80% unchanged). Accumulation risk in renal impairment.
